serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of sambal beef fried rice as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or café, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice with Meat — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sambal beef fried rice in HPB Foodo. Values adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice with Meat' (per 100 g) as the closest Singapore-specific analogue, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for beef fried rice components. Sambal chilli paste contribution to sodium and fat is accounted for by selecting the upper range of the HPB meat fried rice values. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice and fried rice analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal beef fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI ~64, medium category), which is the closest analogous food. The addition of beef, egg, and sambal paste does not substantially alter the GI relative to the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 80.5) / 100 ≈ 51.5, rounded to 51.8 using 23.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving = 80.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving) from soy sauce and sambal paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid sambal beef fried rice due to its high sodium content (~1680 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents approximately 73% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce and sambal is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~51.8 per serving) driven by white rice base with medium GI of 64
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume sambal beef fried rice in small portions or substitute brown rice, as the high glycemic load (~51.8 per 350 g serving) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein may attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and high sodium from soy sauce and sambal paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sambal beef fried rice to occasional consumption due to its combined sodium load (~1680 mg per serving) and saturated fat contribution (~6.3 g per serving), both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; choosing leaner beef cuts and reduced-sodium soy sauce can lower the risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Beef Fried Rice is a spicy Southeast Asian fried rice dish made by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice with sliced or minced beef, sambal chilli paste, egg, mixed vegetables (such as carrots, peas, and spring onions), garlic, shallots, and soy sauce in a hot wok. The dish is characterised by its bold, spicy, and slightly smoky flavour profile derived from the sambal paste, which is a cooked chilli condiment common across Malay, Indonesian, and Filipino cuisines. The texture is fluffy yet slightly charred from high-heat wok cooking. In Singapore, this dish appears on hawker menus and café menus as a fusion adaptation blending Filipino sambal traditions with the ubiquitous Singaporean fried rice format. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-to-high calorie dish (~175 kcal per 100 g) with meaningful protein from beef (~7.5 g per 100 g), moderate fat (~6.2 g per 100 g), and a significant carbohydrate load from white rice (~23 g per 100 g). Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and sambal paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 64 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 51.8, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal-suitable when beef is halal-certified, and is dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is sambal beef fried rice healthy?
How many calories in sambal beef fried rice?

What is the GI of fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sambal beef fried rice exists in HPB Foodo or any other approved primary regional source. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice with Meat' as the closest Singapore-specific analogue, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database fried rice analogue. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice with Meat' and USDA SR Legacy fried rice components; no exact sambal beef fried rice entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food category. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Grains and staples', which is the appropriate classification for a fried rice dish. 5. Cuisine is retained as 'Filipino' per the input, though this dish as served in Singapore represents a cross-cultural hawker adaptation. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data; no direct GI measurement for sambal beef fried rice was located. 7. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified beef and sambal paste. 8. The aliases_by_language entries are fully consistent with the flat aliases list.
